findings.summary: PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS tuple is defined once in paperless.py and imported/used consistently across all 4 modules that make Paperless API calls (claims.py, tags.py, stale_lock.py, preflight.py). All 13 except-clauses reference the shared tuple. No ad-hoc exception tuples found anywhere.
evidence: PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS = (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError, KeyError) defined at paperless.py:22
13 except-clauses across 4 files all reference PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
No ad-hoc exception tuples anywhere in the codebase

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError) - a deliberate superset of PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS. This is intentional since the daemon loop sits above the worker layer and needs resilience against broader error types including RuntimeError and TypeError.
evidence: daemon_loop.py:106 catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError, KeyError)
PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS is (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError, KeyError)
KeyError was recently added per the noted fix for malformed API responses
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting this as a named constant (e.g., DAEMON_LOOP_EXCEPTIONS) to make the relationship with PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS explicit, but this is a minor style point.

findings.summary: finalize_document_with_error() in tags.py calls client.update_document or client.update_document_metadata without try/except. If the Paperless API is down during error finalization, the exception propagates. This is mitigated by the try/finally block in both workers ensuring lock release, and the thread pool executor logging the failure. Defensible design but differs from the guard-every-call pattern used elsewhere in tags.py.
evidence: tags.py lines 116-147: finalize_document_with_error calls client.update_document/update_document_metadata with no try/except
Both worker.py files call _finalize_with_error inside try/finally, so lock release is guaranteed
Contrast with release_processing_tag (tags.py:81-113) and remove_stale_queue_tag (tags.py:52-78) which both wrap calls in try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS
findings.suggestion: Wrap the API calls in finalize_document_with_error with try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS for consistency with the rest of tags.py. Currently a propagated exception here causes the worker thread to fail with an unhandled error log rather than a targeted error-finalization-failed log.

findings.summary: classifier/worker.py _requeue_for_ocr() calls update_document_metadata without exception guarding. Same pattern as finalize_document_with_error - mitigated by outer try/finally but inconsistent with the guarded pattern in tags.py helpers.
evidence: classifier/worker.py:197 calls self.paperless_client.update_document_metadata without try/except
Called inside the try/finally block so lock release is safe
findings.suggestion: Guard with try/except PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS or note the intentional propagation with a comment.

findings.summary: PreflightError is a proper RuntimeError subclass with explicit raise-from semantics. The swallowed RuntimeError noted in the prompt has been properly replaced - _check_paperless_reachable raises PreflightError from the caught exception, preserving the chain.
evidence: preflight.py:15-16: class PreflightError(RuntimeError)
preflight.py:32-34: raise PreflightError(...) from exc
bootstrap.py:45-47: catches PreflightError specifically

findings.summary: Three bare 'except Exception' clauses exist in the codebase, all in appropriate contexts: two in daemon_loop.py for thread pool resilience and safe-item-summary, one in ocr/worker.py for per-page OCR failure isolation. All log the exception and continue.
evidence: daemon_loop.py:38 - catches per-item failures in thread pool
daemon_loop.py:133 - catches failures in _safe_item_summary helper
ocr/worker.py:162 - catches per-page OCR failures
findings.suggestion: These are all justifiable. No change needed.

findings.summary: Tests are organized in three clear tiers: unit (30+ files), integration (2 files), and e2e (2 files). Unit tests use mocks/MagicMock for isolation. Integration tests use real image conversion + text assembly with mocked OCR. E2e tests use stateful mocks tracking tag state across multi-step workflows. ~736 test functions across 39 test files.
evidence: 39 test files, 736 test functions
tests/helpers/factories.py provides make_settings_obj, make_document, make_png_bytes
tests/helpers/mocks.py provides make_mock_paperless, make_mock_ocr_provider
E2e tests use _make_stateful_client tracking tag mutations

findings.summary: 955 Arrange/Act/Assert comments across 16 test files. Nearly every test function has explicit # Arrange, # Act, # Assert section markers. While not harmful, this is a pervasive formatting pattern that adds visual noise without information - the test structure is usually obvious from the code itself.
evidence: 955 total occurrences of '# Arrange', '# Act', '# Assert' across 16 files
Average ~60 AAA comments per test file
findings.suggestion: Consider removing AAA comments from trivial tests (e.g., 3-line tests where the structure is self-evident). Keep them in longer or complex test methods where they genuinely aid readability.

findings.summary: Worker tests (test_worker.py for both OCR and classifier) rely on stacked @patch decorators, sometimes 4-5 deep. This creates tight coupling between tests and internal implementation details (import paths, call order). The e2e tests partially mitigate this with stateful mocks.
evidence: test_worker.py TestProcessHappyPath: 4 stacked @patch decorators
test_worker.py TestImagesAlwaysClosed: 5 stacked @patch decorators
E2e tests use stateful mocks as a better alternative for full-workflow tests
findings.suggestion: For worker unit tests, consider injecting dependencies rather than patching imports. The e2e pattern with _make_stateful_client is a good model to follow more broadly.

findings.summary: Critical paths are well-covered: claim-verify-release lifecycle (test_claims.py), error tag propagation (multiple test classes), lock release on failure (TestProcessAlwaysReleasesLock), image cleanup (TestImagesAlwaysClosed), daemon loop resilience (test_daemon_loop.py), stale lock recovery, and preflight checks all have dedicated test classes.
evidence: 8 tests for claim_processing_tag covering all branches
TestProcessAlwaysReleasesLock: 2 tests verifying lock release on different failures
TestImagesAlwaysClosed: 2 tests verifying image cleanup on success and error
test_daemon_loop.py: tests for fetch failure, item failure, shutdown signal, idle logging

findings.summary: Confirmed: the codebase uses minimal, informative docstrings that do not restate the function name. Module docstrings are single-line. Method docstrings describe behavior or contracts, not obvious implementation details. No banner comments, no verbose test docstrings.
evidence: Module docstrings are single-line, e.g. '"""Processing-lock tag claiming with refresh-before / verify-after semantics."""'
Method docstrings describe contracts: 'Returns True on success, False if already claimed or on error.'
No instances of docstrings like 'This function does X' that merely restate the name
Test classes have minimal docstrings: 'Tests for common.claims.'

findings.summary: The 955 AAA comments (# Arrange / # Act / # Assert) are the closest thing to AI-generated boilerplate in this codebase. While they follow a recognized testing pattern, their ubiquity (every test, even trivial ones) suggests they were generated systematically rather than added selectively where they add value.
evidence: 955 AAA comments across 16 test files
Present even in 3-4 line test functions where structure is self-evident
e.g. test_returns_true_immediately_when_tag_id_is_none has AAA comments for a 4-line test body
findings.suggestion: Remove from trivial tests; keep in complex multi-step tests where they genuinely help. This is a minor concern.

findings.summary: The codebase avoids typical AI-generated overengineering patterns. No unnecessary abstract base classes, no over-generic type parameters, no strategy-pattern-for-one-strategy. The OpenAIChatMixin is a well-motivated abstraction shared by OcrProvider and ClassificationProvider. ThreadSafeStats is appropriately simple.
evidence: OpenAIChatMixin shared by exactly 2 providers - justified
ThreadSafeStats is 18 lines with 3 methods - minimal
LLMConcurrencyLimiter is 25 lines - no over-abstraction
No abstract base classes, no unnecessary interfaces

findings.summary: Inline comments are sparse and purposeful. They explain non-obvious decisions (e.g., 'Copy first so we don't mutate the caller's image in-place' in provider.py:67, proxy trust explanation in library_setup.py:24-25). No comment-per-line patterns.
evidence: provider.py:66-67: '# Resize large images... Copy first so we don't mutate the caller's image in-place.'
library_setup.py:24-25: explains httpx trust_env=False rationale
document_iter.py:50: '# Already processed - remove the stale queue tag'
